questions.text: To set down such choice experiences that my own writings may inspire me and at last I may make wholes of parts. Certainly it is a distinct profession to rescue from oblivion and to fix the sentiments and thoughts which visit all men more or less generally, that the contemplation of the unfinished picture may suggest its harmonious completion. Associate reverently and as much as you can with your loftiest thoughts. Each thought that is welcomed and recorded is a nest egg, by the side of which more will be laid. Thoughts accidentally thrown together become a frame in which more may be developed and exhibited. Perhaps this is the main value of a habit of writing, of keeping a journal-that so we remember our best hours and stimulate ourselves. My thoughts are my company. They have a certain individuality and separate existence, aye, personality. Having by chance recorded a few disconnected thoughts and then brought them into juxtaposition, they suggest a whole new field in which it was possible to labor and to think. Thought begat thought. The author suggests that the main reason to journal is:
questions.subject: English

options.text: To enable us to recall our finest moments and cause us to rejuvenate
options.explanation: The passage emphasizes the importance of recording thoughts and experiences through journaling. The author suggests that by writing down our thoughts, we not only preserve them but also inspire ourselves to reflect on and improve upon them. The idea is that journaling helps us remember our "best hours" and stimulates us to continue developing and nurturing those thoughts. The phrase "so we remember our best hours and stimulate ourselves" directly supports this interpretation, indicating that the primary reason for journaling is to recall our finest moments and rejuvenate our thoughts.

options.text: So we can remember the untoward sides of humanity and reverse course
options.explanation: This option focuses on remembering negative aspects and correcting them, which is not the main idea conveyed in the passage. The passage talks more about positive reflection and growth rather than focusing on negativity.

options.text: Because in no other way can the measure of a man be determined following life
options.explanation: This option introduces the idea of measuring a person's life after death, which is not discussed in the passage. The author is more concerned with the ongoing process of thought and self-improvement.

options.text: That journaling is the pastime that renews thoughts to modify behavior
options.explanation: While this option touches on the idea of renewing thoughts, it does not capture the full essence of the passage, which is about recalling and stimulating our finest moments.

questions.text: Backbone of amino acid is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The backbone of an amino acid chain in proteins is made up of a repeating sequence of carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) atoms, forming peptide bonds.
While the -COOH group is part of the amino acid structure, it doesn't form the backbone that connects amino acids in a chain.
The -NH2 group is also part of an amino acid, but it is not specifically part of the backbone that connects amino acids in proteins.
The hydrogen atom is present in amino acids but does not contribute to the backbone structure of proteins.

questions.explanation: The backbone of an amino acid in the context of proteins refers to the repeating sequence of carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) atoms, often denoted as C-C-N. This structure forms the peptide bonds between amino acids, creating the primary structure of proteins. Option A correctly identifies this sequence. The -COOH group (Option B) and the -NH2 group (Option C) are functional groups found in amino acids but do not make up the backbone. The -H group (Option D) is simply a hydrogen atom, which also does not form part of the backbone.

questions.text: Which type of bond is not formed in maintaining tertiary structure of proteins?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Disulphide bonds are strong covalent bonds formed between sulfur atoms in cysteine residues within the same polypeptide chain, contributing to the stability of tertiary structure.
Ionic bonds form between oppositely charged side chains within the same polypeptide, playing a role in stabilizing tertiary structure.
Hydrogen bonds occur between polar side chains or backbone atoms within the same polypeptide chain, crucial for maintaining tertiary structure.
Inter-chain interactions involve bonds between different polypeptide chains and are characteristic of quaternary structure, not tertiary structure.

questions.explanation: The tertiary structure of proteins is maintained by various interactions and bonds within a single polypeptide chain, including disulfide bonds, ionic bonds, and intra-chain interactions like hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. Inter-chain interactions are involved in quaternary structure, which refers to the assembly of multiple polypeptide chains into a functional protein complex. This is why inter-chain interactions are not responsible for maintaining the tertiary structure.

questions.text: Secondary structure of protein is found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trypsin is an enzyme that aids in digestion. While it has secondary structures, they are not its defining feature, as its main function is catalytic.
Keratin is a structural protein known for its strong secondary structures, mainly alpha helices, which provide strength and stability.
Insulin is a hormone primarily involved in glucose regulation. Though it has secondary structures, they do not define its function.
Glucagon is another hormone involved in glucose metabolism. Its primary role is functional, not structural, thus secondary structures are not prominent.

questions.explanation: Keratin is a fibrous protein that is rich in alpha helices, a common type of secondary structure in proteins. These helices contribute to the strength and stability of keratin. In contrast, trypsin, insulin, and glucagon are primarily functional proteins where secondary structure is not the defining element. Trypsin is an enzyme, while insulin and glucagon are hormones, all of which have specific roles rather than a structural focus.

Fibrous proteins are elongated and insoluble, mainly providing structural support. They do not participate in immune responses like antibodies do.
While enzymes are indeed globular proteins, their primary role is to act as catalysts in speeding up chemical reactions, not in immune functions like antibodies.
This is incorrect because antibodies are exclusively globular proteins and do not share characteristics with fibrous proteins.

questions.explanation: Globular proteins are spherical and soluble in water. They play dynamic roles in biological processes, such as enzymatic activity and immune response, which includes antibodies.

Incorrect. This option is missing the nitrogenous base, which is a crucial component in forming a nucleotide. A nucleotide is incomplete without the base.

questions.explanation: A nucleotide is composed of a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar, and a phosphate group.

questions.text: Which of the following is sparingly soluble in water:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monosaccharides, like glucose and fructose, are small, simple sugars with multiple hydroxyl groups, making them highly soluble in water.
Disaccharides, such as sucrose and lactose, consist of two monosaccharide units. They are generally soluble in water, though less so than monosaccharides due to their larger size.
Oligosaccharides consist of a few monosaccharide units. They exhibit moderate solubility in water, less than disaccharides and monosaccharides, but more than polysaccharides.

Polysaccharides, such as starch and cellulose, are large, complex molecules made up of many monosaccharide units. Their large size and structural complexity result in them being sparingly soluble in water.

questions.explanation: Polysaccharides are large macromolecules composed of many monosaccharide units linked together. Their large size and complex structure make them sparingly soluble in water. In contrast, monosaccharides are small and have multiple hydroxyl groups, making them highly soluble. Disaccharides, although larger than monosaccharides, still tend to be soluble due to their structure, which includes soluble sugar units. Oligosaccharides, which are composed of a few monosaccharide units, have moderate solubility in water but are not as insoluble as polysaccharides.

questions.text: Which of the following polysaccharide produce red color with iodine:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amylose produces a blue-black color with iodine due to its unbranched helical structure.
Amylopectin results in a reddish-brown color with iodine, which is different from a distinct red.
Glycogen produces a red color when it reacts with iodine due to its highly branched structure.
Cellulose does not change color with iodine, as its structure does not allow for interaction in the same way.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Glycogen, which produces a red color when treated with iodine. This color reaction is due to the branching structure of glycogen. Amylose, on the other hand, gives a blue-black color, while amylopectin results in a reddish-brown. Cellulose does not react with iodine to give a color change, due to its different structural properties. Understanding these color reactions can help in identifying the type of polysaccharide present.

questions.text: Antibodies consist of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: 2 light chains and 2 heavy chains arranged in a Y-shaped configuration
options.explanation: This is the correct structure of an antibody. Antibodies, or immunoglobulins, are composed of two identical light chains and two identical heavy chains, forming a Y-shape.

options.text: A light chain and 2 heavy chains arranged in a Y-shaped configuration
options.explanation: This is incorrect because antibodies have two light chains, not one.

options.text: 2 light chains and a heavy chain arranged in a Y-shaped configuration
options.explanation: This is incorrect because antibodies have two heavy chains, not one.
This option is incorrect because only one of the previous options describes the correct structure of antibodies.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Antibodies consist of 2 light chains and 2 heavy chains arranged in a Y-shaped configuration. This structure is crucial for their function in the immune response, allowing them to bind to antigens. Option B is incorrect because antibodies do not consist of one light chain; they have two. Option C is incorrect because antibodies do not consist of one heavy chain; they have two. Option D is incorrect because not all options are correct; only Option A accurately describes the structure of antibodies.

questions.text: What type of immunity is achieved by injecting antibodies, antiserum, anti-venom
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active immunity occurs when the body's immune system is stimulated to produce its own antibodies, typically through exposure to a pathogen or through vaccination.
Passive immunity is achieved by introducing antibodies from an external source, such as through an injection of antiserum or antivenom. This provides immediate but temporary protection.
Innate immunity is the body's natural defense mechanism that we are born with, and it does not involve the introduction of external antibodies.

Naturally induced immunity typically refers to immunity gained through natural exposure to a pathogen, leading to the body's production of antibodies. This is different from receiving antibodies via injection.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Passive immunity. This type of immunity is conferred by the introduction of antibodies from an external source, such as through injections of antiserum or antivenom. This provides immediate protection but is temporary, as it does not involve the activation of the host's immune system to produce its own antibodies. Active immunity, on the other hand, involves the body's own immune response to a pathogen or vaccine. Innate immunity is a natural, non-specific defense mechanism present from birth. Naturally induced immunity involves the body's response to actual exposure to a pathogen, leading to the development of memory cells and long-term protection.

questions.text: Which of the following are said to be the antigen presented cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Melanocytes are primarily involved in the production of melanin, the pigment that gives skin its color. They do not play a role in antigen presentation.
Dendritic cells are key antigen-presenting cells that process antigen material and present it on their surface to T cells, playing a crucial role in initiating the immune response.
Keratinocytes are the primary cells in the epidermis and are responsible for forming a protective barrier. They do not typically present antigens to T cells.
Plasma cells are derived from B cells and are responsible for producing antibodies, not for presenting antigens.

questions.explanation: Dendritic cells are specialized antigen-presenting cells that play a crucial role in the immune system. They capture antigens from pathogens and present them on their surface to T cells, thereby initiating the adaptive immune response. Melanocytes and keratinocytes are primarily associated with skin functions and do not participate in antigen presentation. Plasma cells are involved in antibody production, not in antigen presentation.

questions.text: The type of Immunity in which antibodies are passed from one Individual to another is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Passive immunity occurs when antibodies are transferred from one individual to another, such as through breast milk or antibody injections.
Artificial active immunity is developed through vaccination, where an individual is exposed to a weakened or inactive form of a pathogen to stimulate an immune response.
Natural active immunity is acquired when a person is exposed to a pathogen and their immune system responds by creating antibodies.
Humoral immunity involves antibody production by B cells in response to antigens, but it does not specifically refer to the transfer of antibodies from one individual to another.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Passive Immunity. Passive immunity involves the direct transfer of antibodies from one individual to another. This can occur naturally, such as the transfer of antibodies from mother to child through breast milk, or artificially, such as through antibody injections. In contrast, Artificial Active Immunity and Natural Active Immunity both involve the body's own immune response to antigens, leading to the production of antibodies. Humoral Immunity refers to the aspect of immunity that is mediated by secreted antibodies, but it is not specific to the transfer of antibodies between individuals.

questions.text: What forms the basis of DNA fingerprinting?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The relative proportions of purines and pyrimidines in DNA
options.explanation: DNA fingerprinting is not based on the relative proportions of purines (adenine and guanine) and pyrimidines (cytosine and thymine) in DNA. It focuses on specific DNA sequences.

options.text: The relative difference in the DNA occurrence in blood, skin, and saliva
options.explanation: While DNA can be obtained from blood, skin, and saliva for DNA fingerprinting, this option does not directly address the basis of DNA fingerprinting, which involves analyzing specific DNA sequences, not the source of the DNA.

options.text: The relative amount of DNA in the ridges and grooves of the fingerprints
options.explanation: This option is not related to the basis of DNA fingerprinting. It seems to refer to the physical impressions of fingerprints rather than the genetic analysis of DNA.

options.text: Satellite DNA occurring as highly repeated, short DNA segments
options.explanation: The basis of DNA fingerprinting is VNTR. VNTR is a satellite DNA as a probe showing a high degree of polymorphism. This implies that there are repetitive sequences of DNA that are found across individuals of all species. Scientists identify variations in these sequences.

questions.explanation: DNA fingerprinting is a technique of determining nucleotide sequences of certain areas of DNA which are unique to each individual. The difference of about 0.1% or 3 × 106 base pairs (out of 3 × 10 9 bp) provides individuality to each human being. The human genome possesses numerous small noncoding but inheritable sequences of bases that are repeated many times. These sequences occur near telomere, centromeres, Y chromosome, and heterochromatic area. The area with the same sequence of bases repeated several times is called repetitive DNA. It is separated as a satellite from the bulk DNA during density gradient centrifugation and hence called satellite DNA where repetition of the bases is in tandem. Satellite DNAs show polymorphism (the occurrence of mutations in a population at high frequency), which is the basis of genetic mapping of human genome as well as DNA fingerprinting. While mutations in genes produce alleles with different expressions, mutations in noncoding repetitive DNA have no immediate impact. These mutations which have piled up with time form the basis of polymorphism.

questions.text: In a genetic cross having recessive epistasis, F2 phenotypic ratio would be
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This ratio represents a form of genetic interaction, but not recessive epistasis. It is typically seen in duplicate gene interaction where two genes produce the same phenotype.
This ratio is indicative of duplicate dominant epistasis, not recessive epistasis. It occurs when either of two dominant alleles can produce a particular phenotype.

This is the correct phenotypic ratio for a genetic cross exhibiting recessive epistasis. This occurs when the presence of two recessive alleles masks the effect of alleles at another locus. An example is coat color in mice, where 'bb' masks the expression of 'A/a'.
This ratio is typical of dominant epistasis, not recessive epistasis. In dominant epistasis, the presence of a single dominant allele masks the effect of alleles at another locus.

questions.explanation: The correct phenotypic ratio for recessive epistasis in a genetic cross is 9 : 3 : 4. Recessive epistasis occurs when the presence of two recessive alleles at one locus (e.g., 'bb') can mask the expression of alleles at another locus (e.g., 'A/a'). An example of this is seen in the coat color of mice, where the 'bb' genotype results in an albino phenotype, irrespective of the alleles present at the 'A/a' locus. Option A (9 : 6 : 1) is incorrect, as it represents a duplicate gene interaction. Option B (15 : 1) is incorrect, as it corresponds to duplicate dominant epistasis. Option D (12 : 3 : 1) is incorrect, as it is indicative of dominant epistasis.

questions.text: A colour blind girl is rare because she will be born only when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Her mother and maternal grandfather were colour blind
options.explanation: If the mother and maternal grandfather were colour blind, the daughter would receive one Xc from her mother. However, she needs another Xc from her father to be colour blind. This option does not guarantee that the father is colour blind, making it incorrect.

options.text: Her father and maternal grandfather were colour blind
options.explanation: A colour blind girl is rare because she will be born only when her father and maternal grandfather are colour blind. The father must pass on an Xc chromosome, and the mother must be a carrier or affected for the daughter to express the trait.

options.text: Her mother is colour blind and father has normal vision
options.explanation: If the mother is colour blind, she will pass on an Xc chromosome. However, for the daughter to be colour blind, she must also receive an Xc from her father. This option does not indicate that the father is a carrier or affected, making it incorrect.

options.text: Parents have normal vision but grandparents were colour blind
options.explanation: If both parents have normal vision, it suggests they do not express the colour blindness trait. Both would need to be carriers to pass on the condition to their daughter. Grandparents being colour blind increases carrier likelihood but does not guarantee it, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct option is B: Her father and maternal grandfather were colour blind. Color blindness is an X-linked recessive disorder, meaning a female must inherit two copies of the recessive allele (Xc) to express the trait. In this scenario, the daughter's father must be colour blind (XcY) to pass on an Xc, and the mother must at least be a carrier (XcX) or colour blind (XcXc) to pass on the second Xc. The maternal grandfather's condition indicates that the mother has at least one Xc, fulfilling the condition.Option A is incorrect because it does not ensure the father passes on an Xc. Option C is incorrect as it lacks information on the father's Xc contribution. Option D is incorrect because normal vision parents are less likely to pass two Xc alleles, despite the grandparents' condition.

questions.text: Multiple alleles control inheritance of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a mutation in the PAH gene, involving two alleles of the same gene. It is not controlled by multiple alleles but rather by a single mutated allele.
Colour blindness, particularly red-green colour blindness, is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by a mutation on the X chromosome. It involves a single mutated allele, not multiple alleles.
Sickle cell anaemia is caused by a mutation in the HBB gene and is an autosomal recessive disorder. It is controlled by a single mutated allele rather than multiple alleles.

The ABO blood group system is controlled by multiple alleles: A, B, and O. These alleles determine the presence or absence of antigens on red blood cells, following a codominant inheritance pattern. This allows for diverse blood type expression.

questions.explanation: The correct option is d) Blood Groups. The ABO blood group system is controlled by multiple alleles: A, B, and O. The presence of A or B antigens on red blood cells depends on these alleles, with A and B being codominant, and O being recessive. This results in various blood types. In contrast, Phenylketonuria, colour blindness, and sickle cell anaemia are controlled by single mutated alleles and do not involve multiple alleles.

questions.text: A man of A­ blood group marries a woman of AB blood group. Which type of progeny would indicate that man is heterozygous A?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This blood type results when both parents contribute A and B alleles. Since the mother can contribute a B allele, the father must contribute an A allele. This doesn't demonstrate that the father is heterozygous, as he could be homozygous A (AA).

This blood type can result from inheriting the A allele from either parent. The child could have blood type A whether the father is homozygous A (AA) or heterozygous A (AO), so it doesn't confirm the father's heterozygosity.
Blood type O requires the lack of A and B alleles. Since the woman is AB, she will always contribute either an A or B allele, making it impossible for the child to be type O.

This blood type can only occur if the father is heterozygous A (AO) and passes on the O allele, while the mother with AB blood type contributes the B allele. This combination demonstrates the father's heterozygosity.

questions.explanation: The correct option is D: B. If the progeny has blood type B, it indicates that the father has the O allele, indicating that he is heterozygous for A (AO). The mother, being AB, contributed the B allele.Other options:AB: This combination can happen if the father is either AA or AO, as he only needs to pass the A allele.A: This can also occur whether the father is AA or AO, as either contribution results in blood type A.O: Impossible, as the mother will always contribute an A or B allele because she is AB.

questions.text: Segregation of Mendelian factors (no linkage, no crossing over) occurs during:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Anaphase I is the stage in meiosis where homologous chromosomes are separated and move to opposite poles of the cell. This separation, known as disjunction, results in the segregation of Mendelian factors (alleles) into different gametes, ensuring genetic diversity.

During Anaphase II, sister chromatids are separated, not homologous chromosomes. This stage does not contribute to the segregation of Mendelian factors as it deals with separating identical copies of chromosomes rather than the original homologous pairs.

Diplotene is a part of prophase I where homologous chromosomes begin to separate, but this stage is primarily involved in genetic recombination (crossing over) and not the segregation of Mendelian factors.
In Metaphase I, homologous chromosomes align at the cell equator in preparation for separation, but the actual segregation of Mendelian factors occurs during Anaphase I, not during metaphase.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Anaphase I, during which homologous chromosomes are separated, leading to the segregation of Mendelian factors into different gametes. This stage ensures that each gamete receives a random assortment of alleles, contributing to genetic diversity. In contrast, Anaphase II involves the separation of sister chromatids, which are identical, and therefore does not contribute to Mendelian segregation. Diplotene is involved in crossing over, and Metaphase I is about the alignment of chromosomes but not their segregation.

questions.text: The colour blindness is more likely to occur in males than in females because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The Y-chromosome of males has the genes for distinguishing colours
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The Y-chromosome does not carry the genes for distinguishing colors. The genes responsible for color blindness are located on the X chromosome.

options.text: Genes responsible for certain traits, including colour blindness, are located on the X chromosome, which is part of the sex chromosomes
options.explanation: This is correct. Color blindness is an X-linked recessive trait. Since males have only one X chromosome, the presence of the recessive gene on that chromosome will result in color blindness.

options.text: The trait is dominant in males and recessive in females
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Color blindness is an X-linked recessive trait, not dominant in males. It appears more in males because they have only one X chromosome.
This is incorrect because option B correctly explains why color blindness is more common in males.

questions.explanation: Color blindness is caused by a recessive gene located on the X chromosome. Since males (XY) have only one X chromosome, any recessive gene on it will express itself, resulting in color blindness if present. Females (XX), however, have two X chromosomes, so a recessive gene on one can be masked by a dominant normal vision gene on the other X chromosome, making them carriers without expressing the trait. This genetic mechanism explains why color blindness is more prevalent in males. Option A is incorrect because the Y chromosome does not carry the genes for color vision. Option C is incorrect because the trait is not dominant in males; it is simply more likely to be expressed due to their single X chromosome. Option D is incorrect because option B accurately describes the situation.

questions.text: Egg is liberated from ovary in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The secondary oocyte is the stage at which the egg is released from the ovary during ovulation. During ovulation, the mature ovarian follicle ruptures, and the secondary oocyte is expelled into the fallopian tube. It is in the secondary oocyte stage that the egg is liberated from the ovary.
Primary oocyte stage: The primary oocyte is arrested in prophase I of meiosis and remains in this stage until it is selected for further development. It does not get released from the ovary.

The oogonial stage is the earliest stage of oocyte development, where oogonia, which are precursor cells, undergo mitotic divisions to become primary oocytes. This stage occurs before the primary oocyte stage and well before ovulation.
Option A is correct

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: a. Secondary oocyte stage Now, let's explain all the options along with the reasons: a. Secondary oocyte stage: The secondary oocyte is the stage at which the egg is released from the ovary during ovulation. During ovulation, the mature ovarian follicle ruptures, and the secondary oocyte is expelled into the fallopian tube. It is in the secondary oocyte stage that the egg is liberated from the ovary. b. Primary oocyte stage: The primary oocyte is arrested in prophase I of meiosis and remains in this stage until it is selected for further development. It does not get released from the ovary. c. Oogonial stage: The oogonial stage is the earliest stage of oocyte development, where oogonia, which are precursor cells, undergo mitotic divisions to become primary oocytes. This stage occurs before the primary oocyte stage and well before ovulation. To summarize, the secondary oocyte stage is the correct answer because this is the stage at which the egg is released from the ovary during ovulation. The primary oocyte is arrested in prophase I, the oogonial stage is an earlier developmental stage, and "All of these" is not accurate as they represent different stages in oocyte development.

questions.text: In the 28 day human ovarian cycle, the ovulation takes place typically on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the typical day for ovulation in a 28-day cycle, due to the peak in luteinizing hormone levels.
Day 1 marks the beginning of menstruation, not ovulation.

questions.explanation: Ovulation is the releasing of egg by ruptured graafian follicle. The wall of graafian follicle is ruptured by sudden increase in the level of luteinising hormone. questions.text: Sertoli cells are regulated by the pituitary hormone known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a pituitary hormone that plays a crucial role in the female menstrual cycle and male reproductive system. In females, LH surge triggers ovulation, the release of a mature egg from the ovary. In males, LH stimulates Leydig cells in the testes to produce testosterone, which is essential for sperm production (spermatogenesis). LH does not directly regulate Sertoli cells.

This is the correct option. Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is a pituitary hormone that plays a key role in both males and females. In males, FSH stimulates Sertoli cells in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. Sertoli cells are essential for supporting and nourishing developing sperm cells (spermatogonia) during spermatogenesis. FSH promotes the maturation of sperm cells within the seminiferous tubules.

Growth hormone (GH) is a pituitary hormone that is involved in promoting growth and development, regulating metabolism, and stimulating the production of insulin-like growth factors (IGFs). GH does not directly regulate Sertoli cells or have a direct role in the male reproductive system.

Prolactin is a pituitary hormone primarily known for its role in promoting lactation (milk production) in females after childbirth. Prolactin also has various other roles in the body, including regulation of the immune system and metabolism. Prolactin does not directly regulate Sertoli cells or have a specific role in male reproductive physiology.

questions.text: The correct sequence of spermatogenetic stages leading to the formation of sperms in a mature human testis is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct sequence of spermatogenetic stages leading to the formation of sperm in a mature human testis. The process of spermatogenesis involves several stages of cell division and differentiation, starting with spermatogonia (diploid stem cells in the testes), which undergo mitosis and develop into primary spermatocytes (diploid cells). Primary spermatocytes then undergo meiosis I to produce secondary spermatocytes (haploid cells). Secondary spermatocytes further undergo meiosis II to form spermatids (haploid cells). Finally, spermatids undergo further maturation, including structural changes and the development of a flagellum, to become mature spermatozoa (sperms).

This sequence is not correct. It starts with spermatid (haploid cell), which is a later stage in spermatogenesis, and then proceeds backward to spermatocyte and spermatogonia, which is the reverse order of the correct sequence.

This sequence is also not correct. It starts with spermatogonia (diploid stem cells) and then jumps to spermatid (haploid cell) before proceeding to spermatocyte and sperm. The correct sequence has spermatid after spermatocyte.

This sequence is not correct. It starts with spermatocyte (diploid cell), then moves to spermatogonia (diploid stem cells) before proceeding to spermatids and sperms. The correct sequence has spermatogonia before spermatocyte.

questions.text: The testes in humans are situated outside the abdominal cavity inside a pouch called scrotum. The purpose served is for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Maintaining the scrotal temperature lower than the internal body temperature
options.explanation: The purpose of the testes being situated outside the abdominal cavity inside a pouch called the scrotum is primarily for temperature regulation and sperm development. Sperm production, known as spermatogenesis, occurs optimally at a temperature that is slightly lower than the core body temperature. By being located outside the abdominal cavity within the scrotum, the testes are exposed to a slightly lower temperature compared to the rest of the body.

options.text: Escaping any possible compression by the visceral organs
options.explanation: This is untrue. Scrotum face no threat of compression from the visceral organs. The purpose of the testes being situated outside the abdominal cavity inside a pouch called the scrotum is primarily for temperature regulation and sperm development.

options.text: Providing more space for the growth of epididymis
options.explanation: The purpose of the testes being situated outside the abdominal cavity inside a pouch called the scrotum is primarily for temperature regulation and sperm development.

options.text: Providing a secondary sexual feature for exhibiting the male sex
options.explanation: The purpose of the testes being situated outside the abdominal cavity inside a pouch called the scrotum is primarily for temperature regulation and sperm development.

questions.explanation: The testes are situated outside the abdominal cavity within a pouch called scrotum. The scrotum helps in maintaining the low temperature of the testes (2–2.5°C lower than the normal internal body temperature) necessary for spermatogenesis.

questions.text: Which of the following membrane potential depicts hyperpolarization?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A membrane potential of –50 mV is depolarization, because it is less negative than the resting potential (–70 mV), meaning the membrane moved closer to zero.
Hyperpolarization is when the membrane potential becomes more negative than the resting level (–70 mV). At –90 mV, this occurs due to extra K⁺ leaving or Cl⁻ entering the cell.
A membrane potential of +50 mV is not hyperpolarization — it is depolarisation, because the inside of the cell has become positive compared to the resting potential (–70 mV).
This is the resting membrane potential of a neuron. The inside of the neuron is negatively charged, but not as negatively charged as it would be in a hyperpolarized state.

questions.explanation: Hyperpolarization is a state in which the membrane potential becomes more negative than the normal resting potential of about –70 mV. For example, when the potential reaches –80 mV or –90 mV, the inside of the cell is said to be hyperpolarized. This usually occurs due to the continued outward movement of potassium ions or the inward movement of chloride ions, making the interior of the cell more negative than usual.

questions.text: Spinal cord terminates at the level of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The spinal cord does not terminates at the level of the sacrum. It typically ends around the first or second lumbar (L1 or L2) vertebra and then continues as a bundle of nerve roots called the cauda equina, which extends down to the sacrum. This is why procedures like lumbar punctures (spinal taps) are typically performed below the end of the spinal cord to avoid damaging the cord itself.
The spinal cord does not extend to the coccyx; its termination point is higher up in the vertebral column.
The dorsal root is not the termination point of the spinal cord. It is one of the two roots that come together to form a spinal nerve, with the other root being the ventral root.
The spinal cord typically ends around the first or second lumbar vertebra (L1 or L2) and then continues as the cauda equina. This is consistent with option (a).

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.The spinal cord terminates at the level of the L1 or L2 vertebra in adults. This is because the spinal cord does not grow as fast as the vertebral column during development. As a result, the spinal cord ends up being shorter than the vertebral column. The end of the spinal cord is called the conus medullaris. The conus medullaris is a cone-shaped structure that is made up of gray matter. The gray matter of the conus medullaris contains nerve cells that control the lower extremities.

questions.text: In humans, this part is called as emotion center of brain:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The thalamus is not the emotion center of the brain. It is a relay station that receives sensory information from the body and sends it to the cortex, where it is processed. The thalamus is also involved in attention, sleep, and consciousness.

The amygdala is often referred to as the "emotion center" of the brain. It is a small, almond-shaped structure located in the temporal lobe of the brain. The amygdala is involved in a variety of functions related to emotions, including processing emotions, learning and memory, and decision-making.

The cerebrum is not called the emotion center of the brain. The cerebrum is the largest part of the brain and is responsible for many functions, including motor control, sensory processing, thought and language.

No, the pons are not called the emotion center of the brain. The pons is a part of the brainstem that is involved in a variety of functions, including sleep, breathing, and movement. However, it is not specifically involved in the processing of emotions.

It is a small, almond-shaped structure located in the temporal lobe of the brain. The amygdala is involved in a variety of functions related to emotions, including processing emotions, learning and memory, and decision making.

questions.text: For the impulse to transfer from presynaptic to post-synaptic neuron, the substance required is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sodium is involved in the generation of action potentials but is not the primary substance required for the transfer of the impulse between neurons at the synapse.
Potassium is involved in maintaining the resting membrane potential and repolarization but is not the primary substance required for synaptic transmission.
While various proteins are involved in synaptic transmission, they are not the primary substances required for the transfer of the impulse. Calcium ions are more directly involved in this process.

The transfer of an impulse from the presynaptic neuron to the post-synaptic neuron requires the influx of calcium ions (Ca2+). Calcium plays a key role in triggering the release of neurotransmitters from the presynaptic neuron into the synaptic cleft, which then binds to receptors on the post-synaptic neuron and initiates the transfer of the impulse.

questions.explanation: Calcium ions (Ca2+) are required for the transfer of impulses from the presynaptic neuron to the postsynaptic neuron. When an action potential reaches the presynaptic terminal, it triggers the opening of voltage-gated calcium channels. The influx of calcium ions into the presynaptic terminal leads to the release of neurotransmitter molecules from vesicles into the synaptic cleft. These neurotransmitters then bind to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, transmitting the impulse from one neuron to another.

questions.text: The figure below shows different stages of action potential: What is represented by C?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This phase of an action potential involves the initial increase in membrane potential from its resting state, usually due to the influx of positively charged ions like sodium (Na+). And that phase in the figure is B.

This is the baseline electrical potential difference across the neuron's cell membrane when it is at rest and not transmitting signals. It is the state before any changes occur during an action potential. And that phase in the figure is A.

This phase of an action potential involves the return of the membrane potential to its resting state after depolarization. It is often characterized by the efflux of positively charged ions, typically potassium (K+), out of the neuron. And that phase in the figure is C.

This phase of an action potential occurs when the membrane potential becomes more negative than the resting potential, often due to an excessive efflux of potassium ions or an influx of chloride ions. And that phase in the figure is D.

questions.explanation: This phase of an action potential involves the return of the membrane potential to its resting state after depolarization. It is often characterized by the efflux of positively charged ions, typically potassium (K+), out of the neuron. And that phase in the figure is C.

questions.text: Depolarization of neuron is characterized by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Na+ into the axon and K+ out of the axon
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While Na+ does move into the axon during depolarization, K+ does not move out until repolarization.

options.text: K+ into the axon and Na+ out of the axon
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. During depolarization, Na+ ions move into the axon, not K+.

options.text: Na+ and K+ within the axon toward the axon terminal
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Depolarization involves the influx of Na+ from outside the neuron, not movement within the axon.

options.text: Increased Na+ permeability leading to an influx of Na+
options.explanation: This option is correct. Depolarization is characterized by the increased permeability of the neuron's membrane to Na+ ions, resulting in their influx.

questions.explanation: When a neuron fiber is stimulated by a stimulus of adequate strength (threshold stimulus), the stimulated area of the fiber becomes several times more permeable to Na+ than to K+ due to the opening of voltage-regulating Na+ gates. As a result, Na gates permit the influx of Na+ ions by diffusion. Since more Na+ ions are entering than leaving, the electrical potential of the membrane changes from -70 mV towards zero and then reaches 50 mV. This reversal of polarity across two sides of the membrane is called depolarization. This electropositive inside and electronegative outside last for about one millisecond till the Na+ gates are closed. Note that K+ is not involved in the depolarization of neurons. It is involved in repolarization and hyperpolarization.

questions.text: Which of the following causes the hyperpolarization across neurolemma?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The distribution of charge alone is a general concept related to the separation of positive and negative charges across the cell membrane but does not specifically cause hyperpolarization.
The influx of sodium ions (Na+) tends to depolarize the neurolemma, making it less negative. It does not cause hyperpolarization.

Hyperpolarization across the neurolemma is primarily caused by the late closing of potassium (K+) channels. When these channels remain open for an extended period, excessive potassium efflux continues, making the cell membrane more negatively charged than its resting potential. This increased negativity results in hyperpolarization, making it more difficult for the neuron to generate another action potential.
The outward diffusion of sodium ions (Na+) would contribute to depolarization, not hyperpolarization. Hyperpolarization is characterized by an increased negative charge.

questions.explanation: Hyperpolarization is a change in a cell's membrane potential that makes it more negative. It is the opposite to that of a depolarization. It is mainly caused by late closing of K+ channels.

questions.text: Non-neuronal cells encapsulating mainly axons of peripheral neurons are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microglia are the immune cells of the central nervous system (CNS), involved in responding to injury and disease. They do not encapsulate axons and are not found in the peripheral nervous system.
Oligodendrocytes are myelinating cells in the CNS that wrap around axons to form myelin sheaths, but they do not encapsulate axons in the peripheral nervous system.
Astrocytes are star-shaped glial cells in the CNS that support neurons and maintain the blood-brain barrier. They do not encapsulate axons and are not involved in myelination.
Schwann cells are specialized glial cells in the peripheral nervous system that wrap around axons to form the myelin sheath, which is critical for the rapid conduction of nerve impulses.

questions.explanation: Schwann cells are glial cells in the peripheral nervous system that wrap around axons to form the myelin sheath, which insulates the axon and increases nerve impulse conduction speed.

questions.text: Joint between costal arch and sternum is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibrous joints are immovable and are found in areas such as the skull. They do not allow for movement between the bones.
Cartilaginous joints allow very limited movement and are found in the rib cage, such as between the costal arch and the sternum.
Ball and socket joints are highly mobile joints found in the shoulder and hip, allowing for a wide range of movement.
Synovial joints are highly mobile and are found at locations like the shoulder and knee. They include several types, such as ball and socket, hinge, and pivot joints.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Cartilaginous. These joints allow for slight movement and are connected entirely by cartilage. This type of joint is found between the costal cartilages of the ribs and the sternum, providing flexibility and cushioning.Fibrous joints, on the other hand, do not allow movement and are found in the skull and between teeth and jaws. Ball and socket and Synovial joints allow for a greater range of movement, which is not characteristic of the joint between the costal arch and the sternum.

questions.text: Ribs which are only attached to spinal cord are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: True ribs are the first seven pairs of ribs that directly attach to the sternum, so they are not only attached to the spinal cord.
False ribs are the lower five pairs of ribs that attach indirectly to the sternum through cartilage or not at all, differing from floating ribs.
Floating ribs are the last two pairs of ribs that are only attached to the vertebrae and not to the sternum, making them unique in their lack of anterior attachment.
The costal arch is formed by the cartilages of the false ribs, creating a curved structure at the lower part of the ribcage, not a separate set of ribs.

questions.explanation: Floating ribs are the correct answer because they are the last two pairs of ribs in the human ribcage that do not connect to the sternum at all, only attaching to the vertebrae at the back. This distinguishes them from true ribs, which attach directly to the sternum, and false ribs, which have indirect or no sternum attachment via the costal cartilage. The costal arch is unrelated as it is a structure formed by the costal cartilages of the false ribs.

questions.text: Major protein of connective tissues is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Collagen is the correct answer as it is the major protein of connective tissues. It provides strength and support to tissues such as skin, bone, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments. Collagen is composed of three polypeptide chains that form a triple helix structure.
Actin is a protein found in eukaryotic cells and is a major component of the cytoskeleton. While important for cell structure and movement, it is not the main protein of connective tissues.

Titin is a giant protein found in muscle tissue, specifically in the sarcomere. It helps maintain muscle structure and interacts with other muscle proteins, but it is not the main protein of connective tissues.

Albumin is a protein found in blood plasma and is important for maintaining osmotic pressure and transporting substances in the blood. While essential for blood function, it is not the main protein of connective tissues.

questions.explanation: Collagen is the major protein of connective tissues, providing strength and support. Actin is important for cell structure, Titin is found in muscle tissue, and Albumin is abundant in blood plasma, but they are not the main protein of connective tissues like collagen.

questions.text: Which type(s) of cartilage is/are present in our respiratory passage?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibrocartilage is typically found in areas requiring tough support and limited flexibility, such as intervertebral discs and menisci, not in the respiratory system.
Elastic cartilage provides flexible support in structures like the ear and epiglottis but is not found in the respiratory passages.
Hyaline cartilage forms the supportive rings of the trachea and bronchi, maintaining open airways and facilitating respiration.
This option is incorrect as only hyaline cartilage is found in the respiratory passages, providing structural support to the trachea and bronchi.

questions.explanation: Hyaline cartilage is the correct answer as it forms the supportive structures of the trachea and bronchi, ensuring they remain open for air passage. It is the most abundant type of cartilage in the body and is known for its glassy appearance and resilience. Fibrocartilage is designed for durability in areas subjected to heavy pressure, and elastic cartilage allows for flexibility in regions like the ear. Therefore, neither fibrocartilage nor elastic cartilage is suitable for the structural requirements of the respiratory passages.

questions.text: The joint between ribs and sternum is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibrous joints are connected by dense connective tissue and do not allow movement. Examples include sutures in the skull.
Cartilaginous joints are connected by cartilage, allowing limited movement. The ribs connect to the sternum via hyaline cartilage, making this the correct answer.
Synovial joints are characterized by a fluid-filled joint cavity and allow for a wide range of movement, such as in the knee or elbow. This is not the case for the rib-sternum connection.
Hinge joints allow movement in one direction, similar to a door. Examples include the elbow and knee. The joint between the ribs and sternum does not fit this description.

questions.explanation: Ribs connect to sternum via hyaline cartilage, forming cartilaginous joints.

questions.text: The function of the kidney is completely lost, and it is unable to remove nitrogenous wastes in :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acute renal failure, also known as acute kidney injury (AKI), is a sudden and rapid loss of kidney function that occurs over a short period of time, often within a few hours to a few days. This condition is characterized by a significant decrease in the kidneys' ability to filter waste products and excess fluids from the blood, leading to an accumulation of toxins and waste in the body.

Chronic renal failure, also known as chronic kidney disease (CKD), is a long-term and progressive condition in which the kidneys gradually lose their ability to function effectively over an extended period, often months to years. This condition is characterized by the gradual and irreversible deterioration of kidney function. CKD is typically classified into stages, with stage 1 being the mildest and stage 5 being the most severe.Early detection and appropriate management are essential in controlling CKD and preventing its progression to more severe stages. Regular monitoring of kidney function, blood pressure, and blood glucose levels is often recommended for individuals at risk of or already diagnosed with CKD.

"Partial renal failure" is not a standard medical term. Kidney function is typically described in terms of acute kidney injury (AKI) or chronic kidney disease (CKD), as I mentioned in previous responses. These terms help healthcare professionals understand and communicate the extent and duration of kidney dysfunction.

Kidney stones, also known as renal calculi, are solid, crystalline mineral deposits that form in the kidneys. They can range in size from tiny particles to large, stone-like structures. Kidney stones can develop in one or both of the kidneys and can cause significant discomfort and pain when they move through the urinary tract. Treatment for kidney stones depends on their size, composition, location, and the severity of symptoms. Small stones may pass through the urinary tract on their own with increased fluid intake and pain management. Larger stones or those causing significant pain may require medical intervention, which can include medications to help the stone pass, extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) to break the stone into smaller pieces, or surgical procedures to remove or break down the stone. Dietary and lifestyle changes may also be recommended to reduce the risk of stone recurrence.

questions.explanation: Chronic renal failure, also known as chronic kidney disease (CKD), is a long-term and progressive condition in which the kidneys gradually lose their ability to function effectively over an extended period, often months to years. This condition is characterized by the gradual and irreversible deterioration of kidney function. CKD is typically classified into stages, with stage 1 being the mildest and stage 5 being the most severe.Early detection and appropriate management are essential in controlling CKD and preventing its progression to more severe stages. Regular monitoring of kidney function, blood pressure, and blood glucose levels is often recommended for individuals at risk of or already diagnosed with CKD.

questions.text: Ascending loop of Henle actively transport _ into kidney interstitium to sustain its high concentration.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The ascending loop of Henle is impermeable to water and primarily transports ions, not water, into the kidney interstitium.
This option is correct. The ascending loop of Henle actively transports sodium ions (Na+) into the kidney interstitium, contributing to the osmotic gradient necessary for water reabsorption.
This option is incorrect. Urea is passively reabsorbed in other parts of the nephron, but it is not actively transported by the ascending loop of Henle.
This option is incorrect. H+ ions are involved in acid-base balance and are not the primary ions actively transported in the ascending loop of Henle.

questions.explanation: Sodium is actively reabsorbed from filtrate under the influence of aldosterone hormone while passing through the ascending limb of loop of Henle.

questions.text: Counter current multiplier causes gradual osmotic outflow of water from the filtrate back to kidney as it passes downward in the :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The proximal tubule, also known as the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), is a significant segment of the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and forming urine. The proximal tubule is located immediately downstream from the renal corpuscle (which includes the glomerulus and Bowman's capsule) and plays a vital role in the reabsorption of essential substances and the secretion of waste products from the blood.In summary, the proximal tubule is a vital component of the nephron in the kidney, responsible for the selective reabsorption of essential substances and the secretion of waste products. Its intricate structure and active transport processes make it highly efficient in maintaining the body's water and electrolyte balance while regulating the composition of urine.

The collecting duct, also known as the renal collecting duct, is a critical structure within the nephron, the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and forming urine. The collecting duct is located downstream from the distal tubule and plays a crucial role in the final adjustment of urine concentration and composition.The collecting duct is a crucial structure for fine-tuning the concentration and composition of urine. It plays an essential role in maintaining overall homeostasis and regulating water and electrolyte balance in the body.

The descending limb of the loop of Henle is a part of the nephron in the kidney, specifically within the renal medulla. The loop of Henle is a U-shaped structure, and it consists of two limbs: the descending limb and the ascending limb. These two limbs work together to create a concentration gradient in the renal medulla and play a crucial role in regulating water and electrolyte balance. In summary, the descending limb of the loop of Henle is an essential component of the nephron responsible for creating a concentration gradient in the renal medulla. This gradient allows the kidney to regulate the concentration and volume of urine produced, ultimately contributing to the body's ability to maintain water and electrolyte balance.

The distal tubule, also known as the distal convoluted tubule (DCT), is a segment of the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and forming urine. The distal tubule is situated in the renal cortex, and it follows the proximal tubule and the loop of Henle in the sequence of the nephron's components.In summary, the distal tubule is a vital component of the nephron, playing a key role in the selective reabsorption and secretion of substances to regulate electrolyte balance, blood pressure, and the composition of urine. It works in conjunction with other parts of the nephron to ensure the efficient filtration and processing of blood to form urine.

questions.explanation: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is a part of the nephron in the kidney, specifically within the renal medulla. The loop of Henle is a U-shaped structure, and it consists of two limbs: the descending limb and the ascending limb. These two limbs work together to create a concentration gradient in the renal medulla and play a crucial role in regulating water and electrolyte balance. In summary, the descending limb of the loop of Henle is an essential component of the nephron responsible for creating a concentration gradient in the renal medulla. questions.text: The tubular epithelium also secretes substances into the lumen, which is mainly of :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the tubular epithelium secretes hydrogen ions to regulate pH and maintain homeostasis.
This option is incorrect. While hydroxyl ions are involved in basic reactions, the kidney primarily secretes hydrogen ions to manage acidity.
This option is incorrect. While potassium ions are indeed secreted by the kidney, they are not primarily related to the acid-base balance, which is the focus of this question.
This option is incorrect. Sodium ions are primarily involved in fluid balance and osmotic regulation, not in the secretion of substances to regulate pH levels.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Hydrogen Ions. The tubular epithelium of the kidney plays a crucial role in acid-base balance by secreting hydrogen ions into the lumen, which helps maintain the pH of the blood and urine. This secretion is vital for excreting excess acid and maintaining homeostasis. Other ions like hydroxyl, potassium, and sodium have different primary functions in the body: hydroxyl ions are involved in making solutions basic, potassium ions are mainly involved in electrical signaling and fluid balance, and sodium ions are critical for fluid balance and blood pressure regulation.

questions.text: All useful constituents of the glomerular filtrate are reabsorbed in :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The distal tubule, also known as the distal convoluted tubule (DCT), is a segment of the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and forming urine. The distal tubule is situated in the renal cortex, and it follows the proximal tubule in the sequence of the nephron's components.In summary, the distal tubule is a vital component of the nephron, playing a key role in the selective reabsorption and secretion of substances to regulate electrolyte balance, blood pressure, and the composition of urine. It works in conjunction with other parts of the nephron to ensure the efficient filtration and processing of blood to form urine.

The loop of Henle is a critical structure within the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and forming urine. The loop of Henle is located in the renal medulla, the inner portion of the kidney, and it plays a significant role in the concentration and dilution of urine. It consists of two main segments: the descending limb and the ascending limb.In summary, the loop of Henle is a key structure in the nephron responsible for creating a concentration gradient in the renal medulla and fine-tuning the concentration and dilution of urine. Its countercurrent exchange mechanism is critical for the kidney's ability to regulate water and electrolyte balance.

The proximal tubule, also known as the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), is a significant segment of the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and forming urine. The proximal tubule is located immediately downstream from the renal corpuscle (which includes the glomerulus and Bowman's capsule) and plays a vital role in the reabsorption of essential substances and the secretion of waste products from the blood.In summary, the proximal tubule is a vital component of the nephron in the kidney, responsible for the selective reabsorption of essential substances and the secretion of waste products. Its intricate structure and active transport processes make it highly efficient in maintaining the body's water and electrolyte balance while regulating the composition of urine.

I believe you might be referring to the "collecting duct" rather than the "collected tubule." The collecting duct is an important part of the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and forming urine. The collecting duct plays a crucial role in the final adjustment of urine concentration and composition.In summary, the collecting duct is an essential part of the nephron responsible for the final adjustment of urine concentration and composition. Its ability to reabsorb or allow the excretion of water, as well as its role in regulating electrolyte balance and acid-base balance, is vital for maintaining overall homeostasis in the body.

questions.explanation: All useful constituents of the glomemlar filtrate are reabsorbed in the proximal tubules and when filtrate leaves proximal tubules, it mostly contains nitrogenous wastes.

questions.text: In each nephron, the inner end forms a cup-shaped swelling around a ball of capillaries called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bowman's capsule, also known as the renal corpuscular capsule, is a cup-shaped, double-walled structure in the nephron of the kidney. The nephron is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and producing urine, and Bowman's capsule is a critical component of this process.Bowman's capsule is an integral part of the nephron's filtration system, and its function is essential for maintaining overall body homeostasis by regulating the composition and volume of body fluids.

The glomerulus is a tiny, ball-shaped cluster of blood vessels located within the renal cortex of the kidney. It is a critical component of the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and producing urine.Overall, the glomerulus plays a central role in the initial filtration of blood to create the glomerular filtrate. This filtrate undergoes further processing in the renal tubules, where essential substances are reabsorbed, and waste products are eliminated to produce urine. The glomerulus is a key component of the nephron's filtration system and is essential for maintaining overall body homeostasis.

The Loop of Henle, also known as the nephron loop, is a U-shaped portion of the nephron, which is the functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtering blood and producing urine. The Loop of Henle is located in the renal medulla, the inner region of the kidney.The Loop of Henle is a critical part of the nephron's anatomy and physiology. Its ability to reabsorb water and regulate the concentration of urine is vital for maintaining overall body homeostasis, especially in situations of water conservation or excess water loss.

The renal pelvis is a central cavity within the kidney that serves as a reservoir for urine. It is part of the renal collecting system, which is responsible for collecting and transporting urine from the nephrons (the functional units of the kidney) to the urinary bladder. The renal pelvis is an important structure in the urinary system. The renal pelvis is a critical structure that serves as a conduit for urine transportation from the kidney to the bladder. Its shape and function are essential for the efficient collection and elimination of waste and excess substances from the body.

questions.explanation: The ball of capillaries is called the glomerulus, which is surrounded by a cup-shaped structure called Bowman's capsule. The glomerulus circulates the blood within the cup-shaped Bowman's capsule.

questions.text: In ureotelic animals, urea is formed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Krebs cycle is involved in oxidative phosphorylation, not urea formation
EMP pathway is another name for glycolysis.
Ornithine cycle also known as urea cycle is responsible for urea production from ammonia.
Cori cycle occurs when muscles need energy, not for urea formation.

questions.explanation: Urea is formed of two molecules of ammonia and one molecule of CO2 . During catabolism, proteins break up into amino acids. The amino acids are converted into ketoacids (such as alpha ketoglutaric acid) with the removal of ammonia (deamination). The keto acid enters the Krebs cycle to yield energy. Carbon dioxide is formed during decarboxylation. Ornithine (amino acid) combines with one molecule of NH3 and CO2 producing citrulline (amino acid) and water. Citrulline combines with another molecule of ammonia and forms arginine (essential amino acid) and water. Arginine is broken into urea and ornithine in the presence of an enzyme arginase and water. Thus the cycle is repeated. This is called urea or ornithine or KrebsHenseleit cycle. Most of the urea is produced in the liver. The liver cells continuously release urea into the blood and kidneys withdraw it from the blood to excrete it in urine.

options.explanation: During prolonged fasting, the body breaks down fats into fatty acids, which are converted in the liver into ketone bodies. Excess ketones appear in urine (ketonuria), not fats.

During prolonged fasting, amino acids are mostly used for gluconeogenesis in the liver, and their nitrogen is converted to urea for excretion. Large amounts of amino acids in urine are typically not found unless there is a kidney disorder; instead, find excess ketone bodies in urine are found (ketonuria).

In prolonged fasting, blood glucose is low and conserved for vital organs, so the kidneys don't excrete glucose. Instead, fat breakdown leads to ketone body formation, and these ketones appear in urine (ketonuria), not glucose.
In prolonged fasting, fats are broken down into fatty acids, which are converted in the liver into ketone bodies. Excess ketones accumulate in blood (ketosis) and are excreted in urine (ketonuria).

questions.explanation: During prolonged fasting, the body relies on fat stores for energy, leading to increased fatty acid oxidation in the liver. This process produces ketone bodies such as acetoacetate, b-hydroxybutyrate, and acetone. The presence of excess ketone bodies in urine is termed as ketonuria, indicating a state of increased fat metabolism. While fats, amino acids, and glucose can also be present in urine under certain conditions, the abnormal quantities of ketones in the urine during prolonged fasting specifically point towards increased fatty acid oxidation and the risk of diabetic ketoacidosis.

Expanding the chest cavity also helps the lungs inflate efficiently. Relaxation of the diaphragm occurs only during expiration. Its downward contraction is essential for the active phase of inspiration. This coordinated movement with intercostal muscles ensures effective ventilation.

As CO2 dissolves in the blood, it forms carbonic acid (H2CO3), which dissociates into bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and hydrogen ions (H+). This increased production of hydrogen ions leads to a decrease in the pH of body fluids, making them more acidic.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a function of surfactant?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Surfactant is not involved in the filtration of blood, as this is the role of organs like the kidneys.It does not come into contact with blood directly but remains inside the alveoli.Its primary role is at the air-liquid interface, not in circulation.Blood filtration depends on nephron structures, not alveolar lining.Therefore, “filtration of blood” is not a function of surfactant.

By reducing surface tension and preventing alveolar collapse, surfactant ensures efficient gas exchange in the lungs. During inhalation, oxygen from the inhaled air diffuses across the alveolar walls and into the bloodstream, where it is transported to various tissues in the body. Similarly, carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism, is exchanged from the bloodstream into the alveoli to be exhaled during exhalation. The presence of surfactant ensures that these gas exchange processes occur smoothly and effectively.

Surfactant is crucial for reducing the surface tension at the air-liquid interface within the alveoli of the lungs. The inner lining of the alveoli is coated with a thin layer of fluid, and the surface tension of this fluid tends to cause the alveoli to collapse during exhalation. Surfactant molecules interact with the water molecules in the fluid and help to break down the surface tension. This action prevents the alveoli from collapsing and allows them to remain open, facilitating efficient and easier breathing.

Surfactant itself is not an antiseptic in the chemical sense.However, it provides indirect protection by maintaining alveolar stability and preventing fluid buildup.This healthy environment reduces the chance of bacterial growth.Surfactant proteins (SP-A and SP-D) are known to support innate immune defense.Thus, while it helps lower infection risk, calling it an “antiseptic” is not entirely accurate.

questions.explanation: Surfactant is a lipoprotein substance secreted by type II alveolar cells that plays a vital role in lung function. Its main purpose is to reduce surface tension inside the alveoli, preventing their collapse during exhalation. By keeping the alveoli open, it ensures smooth and efficient gas exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide. Surfactant also indirectly protects the lungs from infection by supporting the immune defense and maintaining a stable environment. It does not, however, take part in blood filtration, which is a function of the kidneys.

Alveolar ducts are tiny, thin-walled passages that follow the respiratory bronchioles. They act as conduits leading air to the alveoli, where gas exchange occurs.
Alveoli are the final sacs where gas exchange occurs. They are found at the end of alveolar ducts, not directly following respiratory bronchioles.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the alveolar duct, which are elongated airways that follow the respiratory bronchioles. They lead directly to the alveolar sacs and alveoli, where the primary gas exchange occurs. The other options are incorrect for the following reasons: Alveolar sacs are clusters of alveoli found at the ends of alveolar ducts, not directly following the bronchioles. Bronchi are larger air passages that divide into bronchioles, not a subsequent structure. Alveoli are the end structures where actual gas exchange occurs, located at the end of alveolar ducts, not directly following the bronchioles.

questions.text: The respiratory centre which regulates respiration is located in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cerebellum is primarily involved in coordinating voluntary movements, balance, and posture. It does not play a direct role in regulating autonomic functions such as respiration.

The medulla oblongata houses the medullary respiratory center, which is crucial for controlling the involuntary process of breathing. It adjusts the respiratory rate and depth in response to changes in blood gas levels.
The cerebral peduncles are part of the midbrain and primarily facilitate motor pathways and relay motor information. They are not involved in the regulation of respiration.
The vagus nerve is involved in parasympathetic control of the heart and digestive tract, and it influences respiration. However, it is not the central location of the respiratory center.

questions.explanation: All options are explained below:a)Cerebellum:The cerebellum is primarily responsible for coordinating motor movements, balance, and muscle control. It is not the location of the respiratory center that regulates respiration.b) Medulla Oblongata:The medulla oblongata, specifically the medullary respiratory center, is the primary region in the brainstem responsible for regulating and controlling respiration. It plays a crucial role in the involuntary control of breathing, monitoring factors like blood gas levels and adjusting the rate and depth of breathing as needed.c) Cerebral Peduncle: The cerebral peduncles are part of the midbrain and are primarily involved in motor control and the relay of motor information. They are not associated with the regulation of respiration.d) The Vagus Nerve:The vagus nerve, or cranial nerve X, is involved in various functions, including parasympathetic control of the heart, lungs, and digestive system.
